title: Radon Mitigation Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Radon Mitigation Business: Real Costs, Earnings, Steps
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a radon mitigation business: certification and licensing, real startup costs, realistic per-system earnings, and what most installers get wrong.
bestFor: Hands-on people comfortable with construction work who can get certified and build referral relationships with real estate agents and inspectors
biggestRisk: Installing systems that fail to lower radon below the action level, or working without required state certification, either of which destroys credibility and exposes you to liability
whatItIs: A radon mitigation business installs systems that reduce radon, a naturally occurring radioactive gas and the second-leading cause of lung cancer in the U.S., to safe levels inside homes and buildings. The most common method is sub-slab depressurization: a contractor cuts into the foundation slab, runs PVC piping from beneath the slab up through the house or along the exterior, and installs an inline fan that continuously draws radon-laden soil gas out and vents it above the roofline. Many operators also offer radon testing, post-mitigation verification testing, and systems for crawlspaces and water. Demand is steady and largely driven by real estate transactions, because radon tests are routine during home inspections and elevated results must be resolved before closing.
dailyReality: Most days mean one to two installs, each taking roughly three to six hours for a standard residential system. You assess the foundation and radon test results, drill a suction point through the slab, excavate a small pit, route and glue PVC piping through the home or up the exterior wall, mount and wire the fan (often the only part needing an electrician in some jurisdictions), seal slab cracks and openings, add a manometer, and verify airflow. Around the installs, you handle quotes, schedule a follow-up confirmation radon test, coordinate with real estate agents and inspectors working against closing deadlines, and manage permits where required. It is physical construction work, drilling, lifting, working in basements and crawlspaces, combined with technical knowledge of building science and airflow.

startupCostsItemized.item: State certification / license + national proficiency credential (NRPP or NRSB)
Hammer drill / core drill and concrete tools
Vacuum/dust extraction and PPE (radon and silica safety)
Initial inventory of PVC pipe, fans, fittings, sealant, manometers
Radon test kits / continuous radon monitors
Business registration / LLC and bonding (where required)

realEarnings.yearOne: Most new operators earn $4,000 to $8,000 per month once certified and getting referrals. A standard residential mitigation system is commonly priced at $800 to $2,500 installed (materials often run $300 to $600), and a solo installer doing one to two systems a day on busy weeks can build meaningful revenue, though early on the schedule is uneven while referral relationships develop.
realEarnings.experienced: Operators with two-plus years, strong agent and inspector referral networks, and efficient installs commonly report $9,000 to $16,000 per month solo or with one helper. Adding testing, commercial buildings, and new-construction radon-resistant rough-ins raises the ceiling.
realEarnings.topEarners: Top operators run multiple crews across a region, hold relationships with many real estate offices and home inspectors, and gross $400k to $1M+ per year. Reaching that requires hiring and training certified installers, managing scheduling against constant closing deadlines, and shifting from installing to running a company.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Referral relationships with real estate agents and home inspectors drive volume more than anything, because most jobs come from transactions. After that, install efficiency, getting systems to pass the post-test the first time, and your local radon geology (some regions have far more elevated homes) determine income.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rate for a solo installer is often $60 to $150 per hour of actual install work, before driving, quoting, and follow-up testing. Counting all unpaid time, blended rates commonly land around $45 to $100 per hour.

howToStart: Month 1: Get certified. Most states either require state radon mitigation licensing/certification or recognize a national proficiency credential (NRPP from AARST or NRSB). Complete the required mitigation course and exam, this is mandatory in many states and expected by agents and inspectors everywhere. Verify your exact state and local rules.

Month 1-2: Learn the EPA-recognized standards (ANSI/AARST mitigation standards), and apprentice with or shadow an experienced installer if possible. Set up your business, liability insurance, and a stocked vehicle of tools and materials.

Month 2: Build referral relationships with home inspectors and real estate agents, who encounter elevated radon results daily during transactions. Offer fast turnaround, since deals are time-sensitive. Get on local radon professional directories.

Months 2-4: Complete your first installs, and confirm each one passes a post-mitigation radon test below the EPA 4.0 pCi/L action level. A flawless pass record is your best marketing with the inspectors who refer you.

Days 120-180: Add services like radon testing, crawlspace and commercial systems, and new-construction rough-ins, and decide whether to hire and train a second certified installer as referral volume grows.

mustHave: Comfort with hands-on construction, drilling concrete, cutting and gluing PVC, working in basements and crawlspaces
Willingness and ability to obtain state certification and the NRPP/NRSB credential
Basic understanding of building science and airflow, since the system must actually lower radon levels
canLearn: Proper sub-slab depressurization design and suction-point placement for different foundations
Radon testing protocols and reading continuous radon monitors
Sales and quoting to homeowners and coordinating with agents on closing timelines
separators: A high first-pass success rate so systems clear the action level without callbacks
Deep referral relationships with inspectors and real estate offices that supply steady transaction-driven work
Clean, code-compliant, good-looking installs that protect your reputation and justify premium pricing

whatPeopleGetWrong: Working without required state certification or the recognized credential, which is illegal in many states and disqualifies them from referrals
Designing systems poorly so radon stays above the action level, forcing costly callbacks and destroying inspector trust
Ignoring silica dust and radon safety practices during slab drilling, creating real health hazards
Sloppy, ugly exterior pipe runs and improper venting that fail code and turn off homeowners and agents
Underestimating how transaction-driven and deadline-sensitive the work is, then missing closing windows
Skipping the post-mitigation verification test, leaving them unable to prove the system actually worked

toolsAndEquipment.item: Hammer drill / core drill and concrete tools
toolsAndEquipment.note: For cutting the suction point through the slab. The core tool of the trade; pair with dust control.
The active component that depressurizes beneath the slab. Stock a few sizes for different system demands.

toolsAndEquipment.item: PVC pipe, fittings, sealant, and manometer
toolsAndEquipment.note: Standard 3-4 inch PVC routes the gas; the manometer (U-tube gauge) shows the system is running.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Continuous radon monitor and test kits
toolsAndEquipment.note: For pre-assessment and required post-mitigation verification testing below 4.0 pCi/L.
Silica dust from slab drilling is a serious hazard; proper extraction and respirators are essential.
Carries tools, pipe, and fans between jobs. Used vehicle is fine to start.

methods: Referral relationships with home inspectors, who find elevated radon during inspections and need a mitigator fast
Relationships with real estate agents and offices handling transactions where radon must be resolved before closing
Listing on national radon professional directories (AARST/NRPP) and your state's certified-contractor list
A Google Business Profile and website targeting 'radon mitigation [city]' with reviews and pass-rate proof
Partnering with HVAC, home inspection, and real estate professionals for cross-referrals
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Homeowners are concentrated where the EPA radon zone maps show elevated soil radon, and most demand surfaces during home sales when inspections flag high levels. Inspectors and agents are the gatekeepers to that work.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Once certified, first jobs often come within a month or two of building inspector and agent referrals. A steady, transaction-fed pipeline usually takes four to eight months of proving fast, passing installs.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad consumer advertising and discount-driven marketing are mostly wasted because demand is event-driven and referral-based. A flashy brand before you have a clean pass record and inspector relationships does little; reliability and results are what generate referrals.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes. In a region with elevated radon and active real estate, a certified solo installer can readily build a full-time income from transaction-driven referrals, capped mainly by how many systems one person can install per day.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Achievable but requires hiring and certifying additional installers and managing a schedule that constantly bends to closing deadlines. Stepping back depends on a trustworthy lead installer and tight quality control, since one failed system reflects on the whole company.
howItScales.sellable: A certified company with strong referral relationships, documented systems, a clean pass record, and recurring agent/inspector channels is sellable for a meaningful multiple of profit. A pure solo operation tied to personal relationships is harder to transfer.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Certified additional installers, reliable scheduling against deadlines, standardized install quality, marketing to a wider network of inspectors and agents, and possibly expanding into testing and commercial work. Going from a one-person install schedule to a team of certified installers is where most mitigation operators stall.

strongFit: You are comfortable with physical construction and willing to get certified
You can build and maintain referral relationships with inspectors and agents
You can respond fast to time-sensitive, transaction-driven jobs
You live in or near a region with elevated radon and active real estate
poorFit: You want a low-skill, no-certification, or fully passive business
You dislike construction work, drilling, and working in basements and crawlspaces
You are unwilling to follow standards and verify results with post-mitigation testing
You are in a low-radon area with little home-sale volume
askYourself: Am I willing to get certified and learn the building science well enough that my systems actually pass?
Can I build the inspector and agent relationships that supply most of the work?
Is radon prevalent and is the real estate market active enough in my area to support steady demand?

faqs.question: Do I need a license or certification to do radon mitigation?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Many states license or certify radon mitigation contractors directly, and most others recognize a national proficiency credential, NRPP (administered by AARST) or NRSB, that requires a course and exam. Some states require both, plus a contractor or specialty trade license. Working without the required credential is illegal in regulated states and will keep agents and inspectors from referring you, so verify your exact state and local requirements first.

faqs.question: How much does a radon mitigation system cost to install?
faqs.answer: A standard residential sub-slab depressurization system is commonly priced at $800 to $2,500 installed, with most jobs in the $1,000 to $1,800 range depending on foundation type, pipe routing, and difficulty. Materials typically run $300 to $600, so the labor and expertise are most of the value. Crawlspaces, multiple suction points, and commercial buildings cost more.

faqs.question: Do I need to be an electrician to wire the fan?
faqs.answer: It depends on your jurisdiction. The radon fan requires a dedicated electrical circuit, and some areas allow the mitigation contractor to make the connection while others require a licensed electrician to wire it. Many operators learn the allowed work or partner with an electrician for the hookup. Always confirm local electrical code and permit requirements.

faqs.question: How do I know the system actually worked?
faqs.answer: You confirm it with a post-mitigation radon test, ideally with a continuous monitor, verifying the level is below the EPA action level of 4.0 pCi/L (lower is better). A reliable first-pass success rate is your most valuable asset, because failed systems mean callbacks and lost referrals. Skipping verification leaves you unable to prove the job was done right.

faqs.question: Why is the work tied to real estate?
faqs.answer: Radon testing is a routine part of home inspections, and elevated results commonly need to be mitigated before a sale closes. That makes home inspectors and real estate agents your most important referral sources, and it makes the work deadline-driven, fast, dependable turnaround is what wins repeat referrals from those professionals.

faqs.question: Is radon mitigation seasonal?
faqs.answer: It follows the real estate cycle more than the weather, so it is typically busier in spring and summer when home sales peak and slower in winter. Radon levels are often higher in colder months when homes are closed up, which can also drive testing. Diversifying into commercial buildings, new-construction rough-ins, and standalone testing helps smooth the swings.

faqs.question: Can I start this part-time?
faqs.answer: It is difficult to run truly part-time because jobs are tied to closing deadlines and agents expect prompt scheduling. You can start solo and grow, but the certification, equipment investment, and the need to respond quickly to transaction-driven work make it more of a committed, full-time trade than a casual side business.

dataSources: U.S. EPA, radon health risk, action levels, and consumer guidance
AARST/NRPP and NRSB, radon mitigation certification standards (ANSI/AARST)
Angi / HomeAdvisor, radon mitigation system cost guides (reported pricing ranges)
Radon professional associations and operator interviews for real-world pricing and earnings
